utterances: Ever.
And just to clarify, you were under the age of 18 at the time.
Yes.
And was there a law in the state in which you were living?
No. There was not a law at the time.
This was some 20 years ago.
You know...
There was not a law regarding parental notification at the time.
Had there been, I would have done whatever it took to get around that law.
Go ahead, Sarah.
I'm sorry.
Well, that's what a lot of young people will say, even if there is a law in place.
They say, `I have to find some way through this without facing this terror.
And, you know, this caller is quite right.

These conversations--you know, the moment where you have to reveal that you're pregnant and then there's reaction; often, the parent is shocked and often very angry--those moments can alter a family relationship for--in all perpetuity.

It's an unguarded moment sometimes, and all of the issues of fears and rage and guilt and so forth can just play out around the kitchen table in a stormy hour and a half on a Tuesday night, and people may not be able to work on through that.

So again, the message for parents is to think in advance about, `How would I handle that?,' and to try and think through the options, the messages, and the love and care that I think most parents hope to extend to their children.

One of the things I'm hoping to convey is that in some cases--and I would put my parents in this case--they're lovely, amazing people, and there is absolutely no resolution that we could have come to in our family that would have resulted in joy around this decision.
I've seen them react to similar situations with other people, and decades down the line, they're still talking about it, and it's still an issue of contention.
I think...
My relationship with them, I think, has been preserved in part because they were not part of this decision.
It was none of their business and it will never be any of their business.
You know, there's another perspective on this, too.
Sometimes parents surprise their children.
You know, they have feelings about an issue, not just about abortion, but, you know, the big issues of the day.
They feel strongly about it.
They announce at the dinner table how they feel about it over many years.

But when it is their child, sometimes the logjam moves; not always, but often in a counseling situation, you know, the counselor will talk with the young person, saying, `Are we absolutely certain that, you know, your furniture will be put in the hall and you'll be cut off from the family forever?

Parents love their children deeply, and when they are in crisis, which is surely what this is, particularly for the younger teens, after a period of disorganization and, again, sometimes raised in very bad behavior...
Well...
...a number of parents do try and come around.
I'm not saying that's true in your instance.
Don't misunderstand me.
I just...
No. And I did not anticipate being thrown out of the house.
Well--and that's one of the reasons that young people in crisis will often turn, at least initially, to someone other than their parent.

I mean, the parent-child relationship is so intense and so loaded and has so many levels, that sometimes an aunt, a relative, a teacher or so forth is a safer place to go first, and you'll often hear young people say that, `Well, first I told so-and-so and so-and-so,' and then together, that young woman, perhaps, you know, with this other adult, will go together to then talk to the parents.
That's not at all unusual.
Terry, thank you very much for the call.
We appreciate it.
Thank you.
Joining us now is Kay Hymowitz.
She's a senior fellow at The Manhattan Institute, author of ""Liberations: Children, Parents and Kids In a Postmodern Age."" And she's with us from her home in Brooklyn, New York.
Kay Mymowitz, nice to have you back on the program as well.
Hi, Neal.
I wonder, what are your thoughts on the laws on parental notification and a minor's access to abortion?

Well, what troubles me about the idea of children being able to go get abortions without notifying their parents is that it's--I can think of no other area where we say to the government--or where the government says to children, `Sure, you don't want to talk to your parents about this, we will support you and guide you through this without talking to them.
Can kids get a tattoo without talking to their parents?
No. Can they leave school early without talking to their par--without getting parental permission?
No, they can't.

So we're in the very odd position where, in some places, you can--you have to get permission from your parents to leave school to get the abortion, but you don't have to get permission from them or, rather, notify them about the abortion.
Now I should make a clear distinction here.
On the one hand, we have permission or consent, as it's sometimes called in the law, and on the other hand, we have notification.
The kinds of laws we're talking about are really notification.
And it's always made a lot of sense to me that there should be such laws and certainly that they should be considered constitutional and up to the states to decide.
There are obviously also cases where girls come from a dysfunctional or abusive homes.
Normally, these laws have an exemption where they can then appeal to a judge for a waiver of notification or consent.
Is that something that you would approve of?

Well, yes, although I've often wondered why it is that you need that judicial bypass--that's generally the way it's referred to--when it comes to an abortion, when it's clear that, in those cases, in those families, the normal relationship has broken down anyway and many of those families, I assume, are under the supervision of the government in some shape or form already.
So I'm not sure, you know, that there's another way to think about it.
The problem with judicial bypass--and I'm just raising problems here; I'm not saying, necessarily, I'm against it.
Problem is that, as in the case of your previous caller, there are many kids who will do whatever they can not to talk to their parents if the government gives them an opportunity to do that.
And in many cases, the judges will say, you know, I don't really know--`This kid seems mature enough to me; I'll say yes.
It depends completely on the whim of the judge, and that doesn't seem like a great arrangement, either.
Let's get another caller on the line, and this is Laura.
Laura's with us from Northampton, Massachusetts.
Hi.
Hi.
I wanted to offer another perspective.
About 24 years ago, I was faced with an unplanned pregnancy.
I was a senior in high school--similar story to the first caller--living in Texas at the time, and I chose to tell my mother.
Of course, I had chosen at the time to have an abortion, and through the urging of the vice principal at my high school, who was a wonderful woman--encouraged me to talk to my mother, and I did.
I was frightened.
I was so scared, but I did it.
And I got exactly what I wasn't expecting, which was an incredible amount of support and love and encouragement.
And she had actually encouraged me not to have an abortion, and I really thought that this was going to be an obstacle in my path that I could not overcome.
I went to the clinic and, literally at the last minute, changed my mind, and I fortunately had the means to support myself.
I was able to go to college full time and get my degree and raise my child, who is now 24 and is a wonderful young man.
But I had a relationship with my mother that was very different from, I think, most of my peers at that time.
I was surprised at how loving and caring she handled it, and through this process had shared with me a similar experience she had had as a young college student.

And, you know, there wasn't--abortion was illegal at the time that she had this experience, and she'd made a promise to herself that if any of her daughters ever was faced with it, that she would handle things differently.
And so it changed the dynamic of our relationship, and we are so incredibly close.
And--very grateful that I had the courage to share with her.
And now, as a parent of three boys--I have all boys--I'm very, very close with them.
We have a very open relationship, and I know that that comes from really creating that from the very beginning.
Now I know there's a lot that I don't know about in their lives, but I hope that they do feel comfortable sharing with me, and so far they have shared some difficult things.
But, for me, it was a good thing for me to do.
I don't know if I could recommend that it become law, though, for other children, because not every family is as functional as ours was.
So...
We're talking about parental notification and abortion.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And, Laura, so have you talked with your children?
You say they're all boys, but nevertheless it does take two to get involved in this.

But first, if you have questions about the political, military and strategic consequences of last summer's war between Israel and Hezbollah, about the process of reconstruction, and whether the agreements that ended the fighting can be sustained.
Our number is 800-989-8255, that's 800-989-TALK.
E-mail is talk@npr.org.
You can also join the conversation on our blog, that's at npr.org/blogofthenation.
Right.
Well, they know what happened to me as a young woman, and we've been very open about sharing that experience.

And they know that no matter what--I love them no matter what, and that there are going to be times in their lives that they do things that might disappoint me, but that my love with always be with them, no matter what.
And I think that that's an important message to convey to children.
Sarah?
Well, this caller is clearly a woman who has decided actively that she wants to be available to her children to deal with the issues that life throws at you.

And she said, really in such a beautiful way, what I was alluding to earlier, which is that if you want to be the one that your children can go to, you need to have this kind of close relationship that she just described so well so that they do not say, literally, `My mother will kill me.'
Mm-hmm.
And, Kay Hymowitz, as you listened to Laura's story--this is the way it should play out.
I think so.

You know, it's interesting--when these laws were first being generated in the late '70s and early '80s, the reason that people were so upset about them was that they assumed--and I think a lot of listeners are assuming; perhaps even you, Neal, are assuming--that the problem was that there would be parents who would force their children to bear children that they didn't want to have, that you would have a bunch of redneck parents who would, you know, either threaten the kids, their children, who wanted to have abortions, or kick them out of the house and that sort of thing.
Wouldn't have been an issue in my family; my mother would have killed me.
But...
(Soundbite of laughter)
My mother, too, Neal.
Yeah, mine, too.
Yeah.
So there we are.
Yeah, right.
But what I was going to say is that...
But here's the strategy, though...
...there are a lot of stories out there, aren't there?
I mean, this story we've just heard is one.

And these days, interestingly enough, the attitude towards abortion among young people has shifted, so that you see more support for abortion among baby boomers, my generation, than you do, actually, among young people.
Perhaps Sarah has the data on this, but there has been a steady, fairly steady, decline in support for abortion among young people.
